title: Clone Graph
description:
You are given `adj`, an adjacency list describing an undirected graph with nodes numbered `0` to `len(adj) - 1`: `adj[i]` is the list of neighbor indices of node `i`, in the order its edges were recorded. Produce a deep clone of the graph — brand-new nodes connected the same way — and return it in the same adjacency-list format (so the returned list is structurally identical to `adj`, including neighbor order).

**Example**

```
adj = [[1,3],[0,2],[1,3],[0,2]]   (a 4-cycle: 0-1-2-3-0)
->  [[1,3],[0,2],[1,3],[0,2]]
```
starter_code:
def clone_graph(adj):
    # adj: adjacency list, adj[i] = list of neighbor indices of node i (0-indexed).
    # Return a deep-cloned adjacency list identical in structure to adj.
    pass
hints: Build lightweight node objects from the adjacency list first, then clone with a DFS (or BFS) that carries a dict mapping original node id -> its clone, so any node already cloned is reused instead of duplicated.
The visited/clone map is what makes cycles safe — check it before recursing into a neighbor so a cycle in the original graph doesn't cause infinite recursion in the clone.

solutions.name: DFS clone with a visited map
solutions.explanation: The adjacency list is first materialized into simple node objects so the algorithm mirrors the classic clone-graph approach used when nodes are objects rather than plain data. A DFS walks the original graph starting from each node not yet cloned, keyed by node id in a `clones` dict; on first visit to a node id, a fresh clone is created and stored in the dict immediately (before recursing into neighbors) so that any cycle back to this node reuses the same clone instead of recursing forever. Neighbor lists are rebuilt by recursively cloning each original neighbor in the original order, which preserves both graph structure and neighbor ordering. Iterating over all node ids (not just id 0) ensures disconnected components are cloned too.
solutions.complexity: Time O(V + E), Space O(V + E)
